prs.title: [Feature] Support custom callable proposer backend for speculative decoding
prs.author: CynicDora
prs.body_head:
**Motivation**
Currently, the speculative decoding framework in vLLM primarily relies on loading a complete, separate HuggingFace model as the drafter. However, researchers and systems engineers often need to experiment with custom, lightweight drafting strategies (such as n-gram matching, retrieval-based drafting, or MCTS heuristics) without the overhead of loading an additional full model.

Based on reviewer feedback, this PR integrates a `custom_class` proposer backend into the core engine. Rather than using an arbitrary callable function, it allows users to pass a module path to a custom Proposer class (e.g., `--custom-proposer-backend my_module.MyCustomProposer`). The engine dynamically imports and instantiates this class, natively integrating with its `.propose()` method. This ensures full interface compliance with vLLM's internal proposers while providing a robust, programmable hook for custom speculative decoding logic.

**Modifications**
* Enabled the initialization and routing of `--custom-proposer-backend` within the speculative decoding configuration.
* Implemented `CustomClassProposer` to handle dynamic imports, instantiation, and strict `.propose()` interface enforcement.
* Ensured proper tensor shaping (`[batch_size, draft_len]`) and device synchronization for the outputs generated by the custom class.
* Added a rigorous integration test (`tests/spec_decode/test_custom_proposer.py`) featuring a `DummyDraftProposer` class to validate the end-to-end tensor pipeline and acceptance logic.

**Verification / Test Results**
I have thoroughly verified this integration locally on a single GPU using `facebook/opt-125m`. The engine successfully compiles (with eager mode enforced), hooks into the dummy draft class during the generation loop, and outputs accurate text without any shape mismatches or memory leaks.

<details>
<summary>Click to expand the local integration test logs</summary>

```text
(vllm_github) (base) houyuze@LAPTOP-5F50KIQB:~/v

prs.title: [Bugfix][Gemma 4] Clamp soft-token estimate to max_soft_tokens
prs.author: hnt2601
prs.body_head:
## Summary

For extreme-aspect-ratio images (e.g. `3x900`), the prompt-side `Gemma4ProcessingInfo._compute_num_soft_tokens` returned more soft tokens than the HF Gemma 4 image processor's vision tower actually emits (which is capped at `max_soft_tokens`). The mismatch crashed `_merge_multimodal_embeddings` mid-forward and propagated `ValueError` out of `EngineCore.step()` with:

```
Attempted to assign 280 multimodal tokens to 289 placeholders
```

This PR fixes the bug by clamping the prompt-side estimator to `max_soft_tokens` so the placeholder count always matches what the encoder will emit, and adds a small typed-exception layer in the Gemma 4 model so a future engine-layer change can classify this failure mode without parsing free-form `ValueError` messages.

## Root cause

`Gemma4ProcessingInfo._compute_num_soft_tokens` computes target H/W via `max(unit, floor(dim * scale / unit) * unit)`. For very thin or very tall images the `max(unit, …)` floor clamps one dimension up to `unit`, while the other scales freely. After dividing by `patch_size**2 * pooling_kernel_size**2`, the result can exceed `max_soft_tokens`.

Concrete repro for `image_height=3, image_width=900, max_soft_tokens=280, patch_size=14, pooling_kernel_size=2`:

- `scale ≈ 9.02`
- `target_h = 28` (floor would give 0; `max(unit=28, …)` lifts it)
- `target_w = 8092`
- `num_patches = (28 // 14) * (8092 // 14) = 1156`
- `soft_tokens = 1156 // 4 = 289`

The HF Gemma 4 image processor caps its vision-tower output at `max_soft_tokens = 280`, so the prompt has 289 image placeholders but only 280 embeddings to fill them.

## Changes

### 1. Clamp at the prompt-side estimator (the fix)

`Gemma4ProcessingInfo._compute_num_soft_tokens` now returns `min(num_patches // (pooling_kernel_size**2), max_soft_tokens)`. Strict tightening of an existing upper bound: any caller that previously got a value ≤ `max_soft_tokens` is unaffected; any caller that got a value > `max_soft_tokens` was al

prs.title: Avoid redundant AITER MoE output copies
prs.author: jiacao-amd
prs.body_head:
## Summary
- allow modular fused experts to return a direct fused output tensor
- make ROCm AITER MoE return the AITER kernel output directly instead of copying into `fused_out`
- skip NoDPEP `fused_out -> output` finalize copy when `TopKWeightAndReduceNoOP` is used and no shared experts are present
- add focused modular-kernel tests for direct output and copy-skip behavior

## Testing
- `python3 -m py_compile vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/modular_kernel.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/topk_weight_and_reduce.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/prepare_finalize/no_dp_ep.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/rocm_aiter_fused_moe.py tests/kernels/moe/test_modular_kernel_direct_output.py`
- `uv run --no-project --with ruff ruff check vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/modular_kernel.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/topk_weight_and_reduce.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/prepare_finalize/no_dp_ep.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/rocm_aiter_fused_moe.py tests/kernels/moe/test_modular_kernel_direct_output.py`
- `uv run --no-project --with ruff ruff format --check vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/modular_kernel.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/topk_weight_and_reduce.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/prepare_finalize/no_dp_ep.py vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/rocm_aiter_fused_moe.py tests/kernels/moe/test_modular_kernel_direct_output.py`

## Benchmark
ROCm MI355X TP4, `MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M2.5`, random 1024 ISL / 1024 OSL, `--gpu-memory-utilization 0.95 --kv-cache-dtype fp8 --block-size=32 --no-enable-prefix-caching --attention-backend ROCM_AITER_FA --trust-remote-code`.

Higher total throughput is better. Fresh paired sweep rerun on 2026-05-02; all rows completed with 0 failed requests.

| Max concurrency | Baseline total tok/s | Patched total tok/s | Delta | Delta % | Baseline tok/GPU/s | Patched tok/GPU/s |
| ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 4 | 633.40 | 654.90 | +21.50 | +3.39% | 158.35 |

prs.title: [Bugfix] Fix spawn_new_process_for_each_test silently swallowing test failures
prs.author: dzhengAP
prs.body_head:
## Problem

`spawn_new_process_for_each_test` was broken — it always passed regardless
of what the test function did, causing silent test coverage failures.

The previous implementation ran `python -m <module_name>` in the child
process, which re-executed the module's `__main__` block instead of
actually calling the test function. `check_returncode()` always saw
exit 0, so any exception raised inside the test was silently swallowed.

Repro from the issue:
```python
@create_new_process_for_each_test("spawn")
def test_failing():
    raise ValueError  # always passed before this fix
```

## Fix

Serialize the test function and its arguments with `cloudpickle` and
pass them to a minimal child script via stdin. The child writes its
full traceback to a temp file on failure; the parent reads it and
raises `RuntimeError` with the traceback included, making CI failures
actionable.

This matches the robustness of `fork_new_process_for_each_test` which
already handled exception propagation correctly.

## Verification

Tested locally with the exact repro from #41415:

```
Testing failure propagation...
FIXED: failure correctly propagated
Test subprocess 'test_failing' failed (exit code 1):
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 4, in <module>
    f(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/tmp/test_fix3.py", line 45, in test_failing
    raise ValueError('should propagate')
ValueError: should propagate

Testing success case...
FIXED: success case passes cleanly
```

- Failing tests now correctly raise `RuntimeError` with the full traceback from the child process
- Passing tests continue to work normally

Fixes #41415

prs.title: [ROCm][Bugfix] Fix init-time bias dtype cast when gate.out_dtype is None
prs.author: rbrugaro-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose

Fix a regression on ROCm where the init-time `e_score_correction_bias` dtype cast introduced in #39999 became a no-op after #39141 removed the `set_out_dtype()` call, re-introducing a per-layer elementwise kernel on every forward pass.

## Root Cause

PR #39999 (merged Apr 24) moved the bias dtype conversion from runtime to model construction:

```python
self.gate.e_score_correction_bias.data = (
    self.gate.e_score_correction_bias.data.to(self.gate.out_dtype)
)
```

This relied on `self.gate.out_dtype` being set by an explicit `set_out_dtype()` call that existed immediately above:

```python
self.gate.set_out_dtype(
    torch.float32
    if self.experts.quant_method.is_monolithic
    and self.experts.routing_method_type == RoutingMethodType.DeepSeekV3
    else torch.bfloat16
)
```

Three days later, PR #39141 (commit `c8bbe0518`, Apr 27) removed that `set_out_dtype()` call as part of a TRTLLM routing refactor, leaving `self.gate.out_dtype = None` for all DeepSeek/Kimi-K2 models.

Since `tensor.to(None)` is a no-op in PyTorch, the bias stays fp32 while the gate output (router logits) is bf16. The conditional runtime safety net in `_aiter_ops.py` then fires on every forward pass:

```python
if correction_bias.dtype != gating_output.dtype:
    correction_bias = correction_bias.to(gating_output.dtype)
```

This launches the same elementwise cast kernel per MoE layer that #39999 was designed to eliminate.

## Fix

Fall back to `self.gate.weight.dtype` when `out_dtype` is `None`:

```python
gate_out_dtype = self.gate.out_dtype or self.gate.weight.dtype
```

This is safe because the entire code block is gated by `self.is_rocm_aiter_moe_enabled`, and on ROCm the gate always uses Tier-3 dispatch (F.linear via ReplicatedLinear), where the output dtype equals the weight dtype (bf16). If `out_dtype` is properly set in the future, it takes precedence over the fallback.

## Tests:
Elementwise no longer present in Kimi-

prs.title: [vLLM IR] 2/N fused_add_rms_norm and maybe_inplace overload
prs.author: ProExpertProg
prs.body_head:
## Summary
This PR extends the vLLM IR framework to support optional in-place semantics 
(reusing input activation memory for outputs) via a new `maybe_inplace` overload.
It includes dispatching/registration infrastructure and additional compiler passes
to manage functionalization through the compilation pipeline.
Finally, this PR ports the `fused_add_rms_norm` to IR using the `maybe_inplace` overload.

Key features:
- **`maybe_inplace` overload**: Ops can allow in-place implementations to reuse activation tensor memory
- **eager-mode functionalization**: When the default overload dispatches to an inplace implementation,
activations are cloned to preserve their contents
- **E2E Compilation pipeline integration**: New and existing passes manage IR ops through the compilation pipeline, 
from functionalization to lowering and clone elimination.
- **Fusion passes updated**: Now using the `fused_add_rms_norm` IR op instead of 
- **IR documentation**: Added a full doc on all vLLM IR features: registration, dispatching, compilation, and overall design.
- **Unit & integration tests**: IR infra, kernels, compilation, etc.

## Motivation

vLLM IR separates operation **semantics** from **implementation**, enabling multiple implementations per op.
However, some kernels might optimize memory usage by reusing input activation buffers for outputs.
This poses two problems: (1) torch.compile does not like in-place operations, 
and (2) not all implementations of the IR op will have the same behavior.

To solve this, we introduce the `maybe_inplace` overload, which is allowed (but not required)
to alias output tensors to inputs. When a user calls the `maybe_inplace` overload, it signals
to the dispatcher & compiler that the inputs are no longer needed and their memory may be reused for outputs.

## Changes

### Core IR (`vllm/ir/op.py`)

1. **Extended `@register_op` decorator**: `allow_inplace` and (optional) `activations` parameters
2. **New `maybe_in

prs.title: [vLLM IR][Rope] Port RotaryEmbedding and DeepseekScalingRotaryEmbedding to IR Ops
prs.author: wxsIcey
prs.body_head:
## Purpose
Port `RotaryEmbedding` and `DeepseekScalingRotaryEmbedding` to vLLM IR, following https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/38756. For the base `RotaryEmbedding`, keep the `key=None` path out of the IR `maybe_inplace` call and fall back to the existing static implementation.

This preserves cross-layer KV sharing behavior and avoids `torch.library` schema inference failures on optional returns.


## Test Plan
To be tested

## Test Result

prs.title: [ROCM][RDNA3] WMMA paged prefill and split-K decode kernels for ROCM_ATTN
prs.author: JartX
prs.body_head:
[Attention][ROCm] RDNA3 fast paths for ROCM_ATTN: split-K decode + WMMA paged prefill

Motivation

The ROCM_ATTN backend on RDNA3 (gfx1100, RX 7900 XT/XTX) leaves significant performance on the table for both decode and prefill on small / interactive workloads:

  • Decode: the default 2-D grid (num_seqs, num_kv_heads) under-utilizes the GPU's 96 CUs whenever num_seqs * num_kv_heads is small (interactive serving, single-user chat). On Qwen3.5 dense W4A16 this caps single-user decode well below memory-bandwidth.
  • Prefill: chunked_prefill_paged_decode falls back to the Triton context_attention_fwd kernel, which on RDNA3 is significantly slower than what hand-tuned WMMA (v_wmma_f32_16x16x16_{f16,bf16}_w32) can do.

This PR adds two RDNA3-specific fast paths inside the existing ROCM_ATTN backend, both gated so non-RDNA3 ROCm devices and unsupported shapes/features fall through to the existing flow with zero behavior change.

What this PR adds

  1. Phase 1 — Triton split-K paged decode kernel (rocm_split_k_decode.py) A 3-D (num_seqs, num_kv_heads, NUM_SEGMENTS) decode kernel + online-softmax reduce that adds K-direction parallelism so small-batch decode saturates the device. Mirrors the pattern used by TRITON_ATTN's unified_attention but preserves the ROCM_ATTN paged KV layout. Decode-only (qlen==1), causal, no quant / alibi / SW / sinks / FP8 / softcap.

  2. Phase 2 — HIP/WMMA paged prefill kernel (paged_prefill_attn_rdna3.cu, paged_prefill_attn_rdna3_v2.cu) Hand-written WMMA kernel registered as torch.ops._C.paged_prefill_attn_rdna3.

    • v1: 1 wave per block, BLOCK_M=

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Add ROCm score absolute tolerance floor
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
This PR keeps the existing per-backend relative tolerances for the online vision cross-encoder score tests, but adds a small backend-specific absolute tolerance floor for `ROCM_AITER_FA` and `FLEX_ATTENTION`.

The affected failures are low-probability text-vs-text scores on ROCm 7.2/gfx950. Those scores drift by only about 0.005 to 0.006 in absolute terms, but that is enough to exceed the current relative tolerance because the expected value is near 0.1. Larger image and multimodal scores remain within the original relative tolerances, so this keeps the check tight while allowing the observed low-score numeric drift.

cc @kenroche

prs.title: [ROCm][Quantization][2/N] Refactor quark_moe w4a8 w/ oracle
prs.author: BowenBao
prs.body_head:
1. Remove `QuarkOCP_MX_MoEMethod_OSS` and add aiter w4a8 backend.
2. Add unittest cases for rocm w4a16, w4a8 fused moe.
3. Validated locally with

```bash
pytest -s -v tests/evals/gpt_oss/test_gpqa_correctness.py \
    --config-list-file=tests/evals/gpt_oss/configs/models-gfx950.txt

pytest -s -v tests/evals/gsm8k/test_gsm8k_correctness.py \
    --config-list-file=tests/evals/gsm8k/configs/models-qwen35-mi355.txt

```

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Fix and stabilize EAGLE3 acceptance tests
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
This updates the EAGLE3 acceptance length regression test to use ROCm backend auto-selection instead of hard-selecting attention backends. It adds ROCm-specific expected per-position acceptance values for gpt-oss without widening the default tolerance. The Qwen3-VL FP8 MoE case is limited to ROCm TP4 and enables expert parallelism to avoid the unsupported block-FP8 TP shard shape. The per-position check now treats lower-than-baseline acceptance as the regression signal, so improvements do not fail the test. 

cc @kenroche

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Align spec decode logprob test prefill settings
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
This PR fixes a ROCm-only failure in `test_spec_decode_logprobs` for the ngram speculative decoding cases. The test compares logprobs from a regular base-model run against logprobs from a speculative decoding run. For that comparison to isolate speculative decoding, both LLM instances need to use the same execution settings. Before this change, the spec-decode LLM forced chunked prefill with `enable_chunked_prefill=True` and `max_num_batched_tokens=32`, while the reference LLM did not.

On ROCm, floating-point reductions can vary with batch geometry and scheduling. That means the two runs could produce slightly different logprobs even when speculative decoding itself was behaving correctly. The observed failure was a logprob difference of about `0.235`, which exceeded the test tolerance.

This change factors the shared LLM options into `llm_kwargs` and passes them to both the reference and spec-decode LLMs. The only intended difference between the two runs is now the speculative decoding configuration, so the test better serves its original purpose.

cc @kenroche

prs.title: [Bugfix][CI][Hardware][AMD] Fix various e4m3fn -> e4m3fnuz normalization issues
prs.author: mawong-amd
prs.body_head:
## Purpose
This PR fixes various issues in e4m3fnuz handling encountered by various quantization code paths (ModelOpt, online). In particular, on `e4m3fnuz` platforms, `normalize_e4m3fn_to_e4m3fnuz` should be called exactly once on a given FP8 linear layer after weights are loaded.
However,
- Some PyTorch linear kernel-based code paths used during ModelOpt quantization were not calling this function.
- Some code paths used in online quantization methods were trying to call this function twice, once in the linear layer and once more in the linear layer's kernel implementation.

The end result is that the Quantization test group should now be passing on ROCm.

This PR also removes a Quark mxfp4 model that was causing failures in the Kernels MoE test group because it is incompatible with HF Transformers v5.
## Test Plan
`VLLM_TEST_FORCE_LOAD_FORMAT=auto pytest -v -s quantization/ --ignore quantization/test_blackwell_moe.py`
`pytest -v -s kernels/moe --ignore=kernels/moe/test_modular_oai_triton_moe.py`
These tests should be run as part of the Quantization and Kernels MoE test groups in vLLM AMD CI.

## Test Result
The above test groups pass, and there are no regressions.

cc @kenroche and @AndreasKaratzas

---

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Fix GPT-OSS Quark MXFP4+FP8 MoE startup
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
Fix GPT-OSS Quark MXFP4+FP8 MoE startup on ROCm/gfx950 by applying the padding required by the Triton/CDNA4 MXFP4 scale layout, and align the GPT-OSS Quark monolithic MoE method with the current `MoERunner` call signature. The GPQA ROCm config for `amd/gpt-oss-20b-MoE-Quant-W-MXFP4-A-FP8-KV-FP8` failed during model startup while processing MoE weights:

```text
RuntimeError: shape '[-1, 90, 2, 16, 11, 2, 4, 1]' is invalid for input of size 8294400
```

The failing path is `QuarkOCP_MX_MoEMethod_OSS`, used for GPT-OSS Quark weights with MXFP4 weights and static FP8 activations. This subclass still swizzles MXFP4 weights and scales through Triton kernels in `process_weights_after_loading()`.

However, the base `QuarkOCP_MX_MoEMethod` treats `w_mxfp4_a_fp8` as an emulation path for sizing purposes, so it does not apply the MXFP4 backend padding. On ROCm/CDNA4, Triton scale swizzling requires aligned dimensions. For the 20B TP=2 case, the unpadded dimensions produce scale shapes based on `hidden_size=2880` and `intermediate_size_per_partition=1440`; `hidden_size / 32 = 90`, which is not compatible with the CDNA4 scale swizzle layout.

## Testing

Ran the focused padded MoE test file:

```text
pytest -s -v evals/gpt_oss/test_gpqa_correctness.py --config-list-file=configs/models-gfx950.txt
```

cc @kenroche

prs.title: [ROCm][CI] Fix NIXL spec-decode acceptance startup and diagnostics
prs.author: AndreasKaratzas
prs.body_head:
- Use `ATTENTION_BACKEND=ROCM_ATTN` override in AMD Buildkite for the NIXL speculative-decoding acceptance test.
- Make the NIXL spec-decode integration script fail fast when a server process exits before readiness instead of polling for the full timeout.
- Pin this integration test path to `127.0.0.1` and set `VLLM_NIXL_SIDE_CHANNEL_HOST` explicitly so HTTP, proxy traffic, and NIXL side-channel metadata use the same loopback address family.
- Improve NIXL KV-cache registration diagnostics when a tensor does not match the expected block dimension, including layer name, shape, stride, backend list, KV layout, and topology mode.

## Scope Notes

1. HTTP readiness is not enough for NIXL. The failing logs showed `/v1/completions` returning 200, then decode later timing out in `_nixl_handshake()` while waiting for prefill metadata on the side-channel socket. The new readiness check sends the same NIXL `get_meta_msg` request over ZMQ that decode needs, so the test does not start until the side channel is actually answering.

2. `localhost` can resolve through IPv6 while the proxy path is already intentionally bound to `127.0.0.1` for CI. This test now uses `127.0.0.1` consistently and passes that host into the Python test for proxy and metrics access.

cc @kenroche
